coredata.dc:title: Determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by dual-emissive Ag/Au nanocluster/carbon dots nanohybrids: Switching the selectivity by pH adjustment
coredata.dc:creator: Babaee, Elaheh
Barati, Ali
Gholivand, Mohammad Bagher
Taherpour, Avat (Arman)
Zolfaghar, Narges
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: An innovative dual-emissive ratiometric nanohybrid probe comprised of red-emitting a (Ag/Au)@insulin nanoclusters (NCs) and blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) was designed for sensitive and selective ratiometric determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions.The fluorescence intensity of CDs (λex = 340 nm; λem = 420 nm) was unaffected in the presence of the metal ions tested, whereas the red emitting NCs (λex = 340 nm; λem = 640 nm) was strongly quenched by both Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. Interestingly, the selectivity of the probe toward these two ions was simply switched by controlling the pH of probe solution without using any chelating agent. The probe selectively responded to Hg2+ ions at acidic condition (pH = 4.0), Cu2+ ions at basic condition (pH = 10.0), and Hg2+-Cu2+ mixtures at pHs within this range. The respective detection limitsfor determination of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions at their specific pH conditions were estimated as 5 nM and 7 nM, over linear ranges of 20–600 nM and 20–2000 nM, respectively. The fabricated ratiometric probe also showed distinguished fluorescence color changes to visual detection of these ions. Finally, the probe was successfully applied to determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in tap and mineral water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanohybrid probe
Bimetallic nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Ratiometric
Fluorescence
Detection of Hg2+and Cu2+
pH control

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions
Effect of solution pH on detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by NCs/CDs nanohybrid
Selectivity of NCs/CDs nanohybrid towards detection of different metal ions
Calibration curves and detection limits for ratiometric sensing of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions
Comparison of the analytical merits of the ratiometric nanohybrid with previously reported fluorescent nanoprobres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images, fluorescence spectra, and XPS spectra of Ag/Au@InsNCs (A, B, and C) and CDs (D, E, and F). Insets in (B) and (E) show photos of the CDs and of Ag/Au@InsNCs solutions at daylight and under UV lamp excitation (365 nm), respectively.

(A) pH dependency of fluorescence quenching of (Ag/Au)@Ins NCs (red color, 500 nM). I0 and I are emission intensities at 640 nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. (B) Fluorescence responses for (Ag/Au)@Ins NCs-CDs to Cu2+ and Hg2+ in the presence and absence of EDTA at 640 nm. Here a, b, c, d, e and f indicate NCs, NCs in the presence of 1.0 μM EDTA, NCs in the presence of 2.0 μM Hg2+, 1 μM EDTA added in the mixed solution of c, NCs in the presence of 0.5 μM Cu2+, and 1.0 μM EDTA added in the mixed solution of e, respectively. All experiments in plot B were performed in PBS buffer solution of pH 7.4 (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
TEM images of Ag/Au@Ins NCs in the presence of a) Hg2+ (2 μM) and b) Cu2+ (0.5 μM) ions.

a) Ins molecule structure and the -S-S- bonds between Cys amino acids. b) The step by step inter-conversions of Cu2+ ion (i.e., Cu2+(H2O)6) with modeled Au-cluster and reaction of sulfur atoms from –S-S- broken bonds of Ins chain with the free valances of Cu2+ ion of Cu2+(H2O)2. c) The step by step inter-conversions of Hg2+ ion (its complex with water) with modeled Au-clusters and reaction of sulfur atoms from –S-S- broken bonds of Ins chain with the free valances of Hg2+ ion.
Selectivity patterns of the NCs/CDs nanohybrid probe to Hg2+ at pH = 4.0 (A) and Cu2+ at pH = 10.0 (B). All metal ion concentrations were 1000 nM.
Schematic presentation of selective ratiometric determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by dual-emissive Ag/Au NCs/CDs nanohybrid through switching selectivity by controlling solution pH.
Application of the dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescence probe for Cu2+ and Hg2+ in real samples (data are average of three replicate measurements).
Comparison of Dual-emissive (Ag/Au) NCs-CDs nanohybrid as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ with previously reported fluorescent-based probes in aqueous solution.

row.entry: With no selectivity over ions even with the help of EDTA(working in un-buffered ultrapure water without mentioning the pH)
Masking of Cu2+ with EDTA for selective determination of Hg2+ (working in un-buffered ultrapure water without mentioning the pH)
Masking of Cu2+ with EDTA for selective determination of Hg2+ (working in phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4)
Masking of Cu2+ with EDTA and Fe3+ with NH4HF2 for selective determination of Hg2+ (working in un-buffered aqueous solution without mentioning the pH)
Masking of Cu2+ with EDTA for selective determination of Hg2+ (working in phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4)
Masking of Cu2+ with EDTA for selective determination of Hg2+(working in phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.0)
Masking of Hg2+ with thymine and masking Cu2+ with potassium pyrophosphate (phosphate buffer solution of pH 10.0)
With no selectivity over ions(working in phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4)
Selective for both ions, inner filter effect from Cu2+-amine complex and interactions between Hg2+ and carboxyl groups of CD (working in phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4)
Cyanide ion was used to eliminate the interference of Cu2+(working in un-buffered ultrapure water without mentioning the pH)

With no selectivity over ions
(working in phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4)
Switching the selectivity by controlling solution pH (pH = 4 for Hg2+ determination and pH = 10 for Cu2+ determination)

head.ce:title: Determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by dual-emissive Ag/Au nanocluster/carbon dots nanohybrids: Switching the selectivity by pH adjustment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative dual-emissive ratiometric nanohybrid probe comprised of red-emitting a (Ag/Au)@insulin nanoclusters (NCs) and blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) was designed for sensitive and selective ratiometric determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions.The fluorescence intensity of CDs (λex = 340 nm; λem = 420 nm) was unaffected in the presence of the metal ions tested, whereas the red emitting NCs (λex = 340 nm; λem = 640 nm) was strongly quenched by both Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. Interestingly, the selectivity of the probe toward these two ions was simply switched by controlling the pH of probe solution without using any chelating agent. The probe selectively responded to Hg2+ ions at acidic condition (pH = 4.0), Cu2+ ions at basic condition (pH = 10.0), and Hg2+-Cu2+ mixtures at pHs within this range. The respective detection limitsfor determination of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions at their specific pH conditions were estimated as 5 nM and 7 nM, over linear ranges of 20–600 nM and 20–2000 nM, respectively. The fabricated ratiometric probe also showed distinguished fluorescence color changes to visual detection of these ions. Finally, the probe was successfully applied to determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in tap and mineral water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the destructive effect of heavy metal ions in environment and human health, their selective and sensitive determination have always been considered as one of the most interesting and crucial fields of research in analytical chemistry. The Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions as two of the most important heavy metal ions are responsible for widespread defects in live organs; in fact, human body absorption of Hg2+ ions causes toxic effects on immunity, genomes, and neurons [1], and abnormal Cu2+ level results in serious diseases such as Alzheimer and Wilson [2].

Since the existing conventional instrumental analytical methods used for determination of trace metal ions usually require expensive in instrumentation and complicated sample preparations [3], the development of simple, selective, and sensitive alternative methods is still one of the most outstanding research fields. Among the recently proposed methods, fluorescence-based probes have drawn high attention due to offering high sensitivity and wide range of measurement using such techniques as direct fluorescence intensity, ratiometric, and energy-transfer-based measurements [4–8]. In this regard, despite of traditional fluorescent dyes, various fluorescent nanoparticles have been wildly used to develop sensitive fluorescent probes in sensing processes [9–12].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with a number of appealing properties such as high water solubility, high fluorescent quantum yield, biocompatibility, ease of surface modification, and excellent optical and chemical stability, which have drawn extensive attention in various field such as electronics, biology, and sensing [13–20]. Interestingly, different starting materials and surface functionalization can considerably alter the sensing ability of CDs. Accordingly, various CDs-based fluorescence probes have been reported for the detection of chemical/biochemical species and metal ions [21–23]. Besides, another new type of fluorescent nanomaterials called as metal nanoclusters (NCs) composed of a few to nearly hundred atoms with diameters less than 10 nm have received increasing attention. Compared with other fluorescent nanoparticles, NCs present unique luminescence features such as large Stokes shifts and high photostability that come from their comparable size to the Fermi wavelength of electrons [24–26]. Similar to CDs, the metal NCs synthesized with different stabilizing ligands possess different emission properties and can be nicely applied as selective and sensitive fluorescent chemical sensing and biosensing systems [27,28].

Typically, most of the fluorescence-based probes are single-intensity-based probes that are limited by some restricting factors such as variations in the probe concentration or excitation light source [29]. Fabrication of ratiometric fluorescence probes to eliminate these prevalent defects is a desire of researchers. In this regard, various interesting ratiometric fluorescence probes based on both single and hybrid of fluorescent nanomaterials have already been reported [4,6,30,31]. In addition, the design and construction of single nanosensors for detection of multiple analytes is also very affordable in analytical chemistry [9,32,33]. Many examples can be found in which a fluorescent probe significantly respond to more than one analyte, however, with no selectivity over the analytes. A good example for such fluorescent probes is NCs-based probes reported for determination of metal ions, in which their fluorescence significantly quench in the presence of both Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions [34,35].

While, employing EDTA as masking agent has been proposed to masking one of these ions for selective determination of the other one, the problem is that EDTA forms strong complexes with both Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions [34], resulting in the narrow selectivity of these probes. Generally speaking, it is still highly desired to develop an assay for selective detection of more than one metal ion with high sensitivity.

In recent years, we have been involved in preparation and characterization of novel metal nanoclusters [6,7,11] and carbon dots [8–10,12] and their applications to sensitive and selective detection of a variety of important inorganic ions and organic and biological compounds. In the present work, a dual-emissive fluorescent nanohybrid probe is proposed for sensitive and selective ratiometric detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions. Novel fluorescent bimetallic (Au/Ag)@Ins NCs, synthesized in the presence of insulin (Ins) as both stabilizer and reducing agents, and the highly fluorescent CDs, synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine as initial reagents by a one-pot hydrothermal method, were hybridized to construct a ratiometric fluorescent nanohybrid probe (NCs/CDs). The fluorescence of NCs, which responded to the presence of both metal ions, was used as detecting signal, and that of CDs, which showed no measurable fluorescence changes toward the same concentration levels of these metal ions, was used as reference signal. Interestingly, the selective measurement of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions was achieved by a simple pH-tuning method, according to which, the nanohybrid probe selectively responded to Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions at pHs 4.0 and 10.0, respectively (Scheme 1 ).

Insulin (Ins) of high purity was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Analytical grade tetrachloroauric (III) acid (HAuCl4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and all metal salts were obtained from Merck. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water, 18.25 MΩ cm, was employed for all experiments.

Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp as the excitation light source. All measurements were performed in a 1.0 cm quartz cell. The UV–vis and IR spectra were recorded on an Agilent 8453 diode array spectrophotometer and a Tensor 27Bruker instrument (Bruker, Japan), respectively. All pH measurements were carried out at 25.0 ± 0.1 °C by using a Metrohm 713 pH/ion-meter with a standard uncertainty of 0.1 mV (Metrohm, Switzerland). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Zeiss-EM10C transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 80 kV.The XPS analyses was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, 8025- BesTec twin anode XR3E2 X-ray source system).

All glassware were cleaned with freshly prepared aqua regia (HNO3/HCl, 1:3), subsequently rinsed with copious amount of distilled water, and dried well before use. In a typical experiment, in a 10 mL beaker, was added 4 mg of Ins dissolved in 5.0 mL of ultrapure water, and the pH of solution was adjusted to 10.5 by drop wise addition of 1.0 M NaOH solution. After 10 min, 0.5 mL of 10 mM AgNO3 was added and the reaction was allowed to proceed under vigorous stirring in the dark at 37 °C. After 24 h, while the color of the solution was changing from colorless to light brown with a very low red emission, 0.25 mL of HAuCl410 mM was added. The solution was allowed to stir for another 48 h to produce bimetallic (Ag/Au)@Ins NCs. Finally, the solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for15 min to remove any suspended particles and byproducts with large sizes, and the pale yellow solution of the as-prepared NCs were stored at 4 °C until required for further use.

One-step hydrothermal method was employed for synthesis of CDs according to our previously published work [10]. Briefly, 2.0 g citric acid and 1.0 mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 100 mL water. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. After heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the resultant CDs solutions were filtered through a 0.2 μm microporous membrane, precipitated using acetone, and dried in oven at 80 °C.

Following procedure was employed to prepare NCs/CDs nanohybrid system. First, 10.0 μL of the as-prepared CDs solution and 25.0 μL of the obtained (Ag/Au)@Ins NCs solution were thoroughly mixed in 2 mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10 mM). Then, EDC/NHS (25 μL, 5 mg mL−1) solution was added and the mixture was kept under vigorous stirring for surface hybridization through chemical reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence measurements were performed under the same conditions: the excitation and emission slits were set at 10 nm and 5 nm, respectively, the excitation wavelength was set at 340 nm, and emission was recorded from 360 to 870 nm. The typical procedure for the detection of Hg2+ or Cu2+ ions was as follows. Ideally, nanohybrid probes for ratiometric fluorescent detection emit the different emission colors at a single excitation wavelength. Under this condition, the fluctuations of excitation source are the same for whole group of probes, and at the same time all the nanohybrid components are excited. pH of the ratiometric probe solutions was set to 4.0 for Hg2+ and to 10.0 for Cu2+ measurements. Then, desire volumes of the stock solutions of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions with certain concentrations were added to the NCs/CDs probe solutions and their fluorescence spectra were recorded after 2.0 min. The corresponding fluorescence intensity ratio (I640/I420) was considered to plot the calibration curves and prediction of spiked concentrations. For the selectivity and interference studies, a desire volume of the stock solution of other metal ions (i.e., Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Al3+) were mixed with the probe solution under similar conditions and then their fluorescence spectra Tap and mineral water samples, collected from our lab, and prepared from local market, were directly used without any pre-treatment. Different concentrations of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions were spiked into a mixture of 1.0 mL of water samples and 1.0 mL of PBS buffer, containing the nanohybrid probe, and then the ratiometric fluorescence signal was recorded.

The molecular modeling of Hg2+ and Cu2+ cations with Ins capped Au-clusters modeled structure, and also inter-conversion of S-atoms of Cys amino acids of Ins molecule were undertaken by appropriate quantum mechanical (QM) methods. The calculations were performed by Spartan (‘10-Quantum Mechanics Program: (PC/×86) 1.1.0v4. 2011, Wave function Inc., USA). The structures of the complexes of Hg2+ and Cu2+ cations with H2O molecules were obtained by QM-SCF-MO/PM6 method. The QM-PM6 method was utilized, because the construction Hg2+ and Cu2+ complexes cations have been defined and identified by the Hamiltonians of PM6 method. The different structures in this modeling were optimized by the hybrid QM-RHF/PM6 method. A one layer cluster of Au atoms with dimensions of 12.663 × 12.663 Å (with 36 × 36 Au atoms) was applied a model for AuNCs. A part of chain-A of Ins molecule with –S-S- bond and the sequence of amino acids -Cys-Cys-Thr-Ser-Ile-Cys-Ser- (the bold amino acids connect together by SS bonds) was selected to investigate the interconversion of S-atoms with the Hg2+ and Cu2+ cations and Au-cluster layer.

Different characterization methods including FTIR spectroscopy, XPS, TEM, zeta potential, and photoluminescence spectroscopy were carried out on the as-prepared NCs and CDs. The quantum yield of NCs was calculated to be 5% by using Rhodamine B as reference. The TEM images of NCs clearly showed that nanoparticles possessed spherical morphologies with an average diameter of 5.0 ± 1.3 nm, and their hydrodynamic diameter measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) was 7.6 ± 1.7 nm (Figs. 1 A, and S1). The dispersed Ins/(Ag-Au)NCs solution presented a pale yellow color with the main excitation band at about 340 nm, and a dark red emission color with the maximum emission at about 640 nm under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 1B). The shape and maximum of the emission spectrum only slightly changed with pH of the solution. The zeta potential of NCs was -22 ± 8 mV indicating a great electrostatic repulsion between particles that results in their well dispersion in aqueous solutions. The chemical states of Ag and Au in the prepared (Ag-Au)@Ins NCs was evaluated by XPS analysis (Fig. 1C). As seen, two XPS peaks at 368 and 374 eV were detected, which are related to 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 levels of the zero-charged Ag atoms [Ag(0)] [36]. In addition, two peaks at about 84.6 and 88.4 eV are also assigned to the Au 4f levels [37]. Each of these peaks could be de-convoluted into two overlapped peaks. For example, the peak of Au 4f7/2 level appeared at 88.4 eV was de-convoluted into two distinct peaks centered at binding energies of 84.5 and 85.2 eV that are attributed to Au(0) and Au(I), respectively.

The FTIR technique was also used to identify changes in the conformation and secondary structure of Ins protein (see Fig. S2). Both peaks of amide I (peaks at region 1600-1700 cm−1 attributed to the CO stretching vibration) and amide II (peak at 1550 cm−1 correspond to CN stretching and NH bending vibrations) were associated to the secondary structure of the Ins. The variation in intensity of these peaks after synthesis process demonstrate the formation of the Ins-capped NCs [38].

The as-prepared CDs presented an excitation spectrum with two sharp peaks at about 340 and 235 nm, and an intense blue emission with the emission maximum located at 440 nm with a nearly excitation-independent behavior (Fig. 1E). The TEM image of CDs showed nearly spherical particles with an average diameter of about 5.0 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. 1D). The full scan XPS spectrum of CDs shows the existence of carbon (C1s, 285 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 402 eV) and oxygen (O1s, 532 eV) elements (Fig. 1F). In the high-resolution C1s spectra, three main peaks at 284.8 eV (C-C/C = C), 286.5 eV (CO/CN), and 287.8 eV (CO/CN) were detected.

The FTIR spectra of CDs displayed the peaks at regions 3200–3550 and 2800–2950 cm−1 that could be attributed to the COH and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. Stretching and bending vibrations of the NH could appear at 3000–3300 and 1400 cm−1, respectively (see Fig. S3). The presence of CO groups confirmed by the peak at about 1600 cm−1, and the band at 1080 cm−1 presented the existence of CO (hydroxyl, ester, epoxide, or ether) groups. The existence of these functional groups led to excellent water solubility of CDs without further surface modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution pH on detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by NCs/CDs nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, when the influence of solution pH was examined to optimize the sensing conditions, it was found that the sensitivity and selectivity of the NCs are highly pH-dependent. Thus, the response of NCs toward Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions at pH values ranging from 4.0 to 10.0 was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 2 A. As seen, the NCs showed a slight response to Cu2+ ion at pH = 4, but their response gradually increased with solution pH and possessed the highest sensitivity to the cation at pH range 7.0-10.0. However, since the NCs revealed no detectable response to Hg2+ ion at pH = 10.0, this pH was applied to selective and sensitive determination of Cu2+ ion. On the other hand, in the case of Hg2+ ion, the highest response of NCs was observed at pH = 4, and increasing the pH of solution was led to a significant decrease in the fluorescence response. Since both the highest sensitivity toward Hg2+ ion and the minimum response to Cu2+ ion were achieved at pH = 4.0, this pH value was considered as the optimal condition for Hg2+ determination. The results thus obtained clearly revealed that the selectivity of the NCs/CDs nanohybrid probe toward Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions could be simply switched by controlling the solution pH without need to use any specific chelating agent.

The observed differences in the pH dependency of the NCs response toward Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions could be originated from different quenching mechanism of the two cations. According to the previous reports [39–42], the fluorescence quenching of NCs in the presence of Hg2+ ions is a result of metal–metal interactions, while, in the case of Cu2+ ions, the induced fluorescence quenching is a result of their binding to the capping proteins. The high-affinity of d10-d10 metallophilic interactions between the Au+ and Hg2+ ions results in effective quenching of the fluorescence of (Ag/Au)NCs, which turn them into well label-free fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection [40,40,41,42]. However, the observed decrease in sensing ability of NCs towards the Hg2+ ion at increased solution pH can be attributed to the low solubility product of Hg(OH)2 (i.e., Ksp = 3.6 × 10−26). In fact, at basic pH values, the content of the free Hg2+ ions dramatically decrease and most of them precipitate in the form of hydroxide. The extreme condition is pH = 10.0, at which the free Hg2+ content in solution is so low that has no measurable effect on the fluorescence intensity of NCs.

On the other hand, many reports indicate the high affinity of Cu2+ ions to the amino acids presented in the capping proteins [43,44]. Durgadas et al. investigated the interaction of Cu2+ ions and bovine serum albumin-capped AuNCs and demonstrated that bonding of Cu2+ ions to the binding sites (amino acids) of the coating protein is responsible for quenching of NCs [39]. These interactions disturb the passivation efficiency of the capping agent and, thus, indirectly affect the fluorescence of NCs. Therefore, the higher quenching efficiency of Cu2+ at high pH values can be assigned to more available binding sites in the protein chain to interact with this ion [44]. Consequently, at lower pH values, protonation of the amino acids reduce the availability of the binding sites and, consequently, the sensitivity of NCs toward Cu2+ ions. Fig. S4 displays the FT-IR spectra of nanohybrid individually and in the presence of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. The decrease in intensity of almost all FTIR peaks after addition of Cu2+ ions to the nanohybrid solution could be associated to the interaction of Cu2+ ion with functional group of Ins protein on the surface of NCs. From the FTIR spectra it can be concluded that the binding involves both histidyl and carboxyl groups. However, for Hg2+ sensing such a decrease was not observed in the FTIR spectrum, which demonstrated that Hg2+ does not interact with the surface functional groups. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of NCs quenched by Cu2+ ions was recovered when adequate EDTA was added to the solution, but the fluorescence intensity of NCs quenched by Hg2+ ions was unaffected (Fig. 2B at pH 7.4 for both Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, and Fig. S5 at pH = 4 for Hg2+ and pH = 10 for Cu2+).

The proposed mechanisms for interaction of NCs by Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions were further confirmed with TEM images and theoretical calculations. The TEM images of Ag/Au@Ins NCs in the presence of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions are shown in Fig. 3 . From these images, it can be seen that the NCs aggregate in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. 3b) that can be attributed to the interaction between the Ins shell of clusters and Cu2+ ions [38]. On other hand, the addition of Hg2+ ions to the NCs solution (Fig. 3a) has little or nearly no effect on the size and ruled out the interaction of Hg2+ ions with the surface functional groups of NCs.

Moreover, we used theoretical computation to comparing the interactions between the Au@Ins NCs with Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions (Fig. 4 ). Ins molecule structure, the -S-S- bonds between Cys amino acids and the sequence of amino acids that was applied to investigate the inter-conversion of S-atoms with the Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions and Au-cluster layer are shown in Fig. 4a. Fig. S6 shows the reaction between Au-cluster modeled and sulfur atoms from broken -S-S- bonds of Ins chain. Due to the complexation of the broken–S-S- bonds with the modeled Au layer, Au layer has shown a small distortions compared with the Au layer in the net form before S-Au connections.

Fig. 4b demonstrates the step by step inter-conversions of Cu2+ cation (Cu2+(H2O)6) with Au- modeled cluster, and in the next step reaction of sulfur atoms from –S-S- broken bonds of Ins chain with the free valances of Cu2+ cation of Cu2+ (H2O)2. Due to the complexation of the broken -S-S- bonds with the modeled Au-cluster layer, Au layer has shown more distortions compared with the previously discussed form (with Au layer in the net form before S-Au connections). In this case, the dimensions of the Au layer became 10.945 × 10.901 Å (with 36 × 36 Au atoms) so that Au-cluster takes a small bowel form. At this stage, the modeling results show that by removing 4H2O molecules from Cu2+(H2O)6 the new form, i.e. Cu2+(H2O)2, has settled on the Au-layer and the free valances of Cu2+ have reacted with -S-S- broken bonds of Ins chain. On the other hand, the step by step inter-conversions of Hg2+ cation with modeled Au-cluster and reaction of sulfur atoms from -S-S- broken bonds of Ins chain with the free valances of Hg2+ cation is depicted In Fig. 4c. In this case, the distortion of Au-cluster around Hg2+ is greater than the cases for Cu2+ and net Au-cluster with Ins molecule. Moreover, the complexation of the broken -S-S- bonds with the Au-cluster layer increased this distortion more intensively. This phenomenon could be shown by the dimensions of the Au-layer and the high distortion of this layer around Hg2+ ion. Here, the dimension of the Au layer became 8.965 × 8.951 Å (with 36 × 36 Au atoms) and Au-cluster got a greater distorted form. Our calculations demonstrated that the ΔG D of Au-cluster distortion for Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions are 61.99 and 32.05 kcal mol−1, respectively. Therefore, the distortion of Au-cluster in presence of Hg2+ is much easier than Cu2+ cation. The results show that Hg2+ ion has more willingness to react with the Au-surface and then -S-S- broken bonds of Ins chain. These calculations results and the theoretical discussions show good agreement with the experimental obtained results, where EDTA could recover the fluorescence of NCs affected by Cu2+ ions but not the fluorescence of NCs affected by Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of NCs/CDs nanohybrid towards detection of different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In preliminary experiments, a variety of different alkali, alkaline earth, transition and heavy metal ions including Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Al3+ were tested to investigate their effects on the fluorescence intensity of both NCs and CDs, separately, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . As it is quite obvious from Fig. 5A and B, while the fluorescence of NCs was obviously quenched in the presence of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, at corresponding optimized pHs, the other metal ions possessed negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs, used as the reference signal, was almost unaffected in the presence of all metal ions tested, and more importantly, in the presence of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions. Therefore, both of these fluorescent nanomaterials were employed to develop a ratiometric fluorescent nanohybrid probe using the EDC/NHS coupling method [31]. It should be noted that the attachment of CDs and NCs, via the EDC/NHS linkers, shifted the emission wavelength of CDs from 440 nm to 420 nm, but possessed no significant effect on the emission wavelength of NCs. Thus, the nanohybrid probes with two distinct emission peaks centered at420 and about 640 nm, which were employed as the reference and sensing signals, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curves and detection limits for ratiometric sensing of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The above mentioned optimal pH conditions for the Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions were then employed to construct their calibration curves based on the prepared nanohybrid probe. Fig. 6 shows the effect of various concentrations of these ions on the fluorescent response of the nanohybrid probe at the optimized pH conditions. All spectra were then normalized to the maximum emission of CDs at 420 nm. As is clearly seen, the increasing concentration of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions gradually decrease the intensity of the red fluorescence of NCs at 640 nm, while the emission peak of CDs remained unchanged. The inset of Fig. 6A shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio of nanohybrid probe at pH 4.0 possessed a linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ from 20 to 2000 nM with a regression equation of (R0/R) = 0.0024 [Hg2+] + 0.9238, where R0 and R are the ratio of I640 /I420 in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. The detection limit for Hg2+ based on three signal-to-noise ratio (standard deviation of blank) was estimated as 5.0 nM (1.0 ppb), which is lower than the permitted maximum level of 2.0 ppb of Hg2+ in drinking water suggested by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [45]. Moreover, a clear and successive color change from the original pink color of the nanohybrid to blue can be observed upon quenching of the NCs emission, which is favorable for the visual detection of Hg2+ by the naked eyes.

A similar trend was also observed for the fluorescent response of the nanohybrid probe for the increasing concentration of Cu2+ ion to the probe solution at pH 10.0, as shown in Fig. 6B. It is obvious that a linear range for Cu2+ was obtained from 20 to 600 nM with a regression equation of (R0/R) = 0.0038 [Cu2+] + 1.049 (inset of Fig. 6B). Here, the detection limit for Cu2+ was calculated as 7.0 nM (0.4 ppb), which is much lower than its maximum permitted level of 1.3 ppm in drinking water [45]. Here, the observed fluorescence color change for Cu2+ detection, from green to blue, was less visible than that of Hg2+ detection most possibly because of the higher fluorescence intensity of the CDs at pH = 10.0. Relative standard deviations obtained by this nanohybrid probe for five individual replications of determination for 50 nM of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions were 2.5% and 2.8%, respectively.

As discussed above, while the nanohybrid probe intensively response to Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions at their specific pH conditions, none of other metal ions led to a significant change in the fluorescence signal. Therefore, the proposed pH dependence response of the dual-emission ratiometric probe could be utilized for the selective measurement of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions in real samples without any significant interference of other metal ions. To verify this, tap and mineral water samples spiked with different concentrations of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions were checked. The obtained results for the prediction of the concentrations of these toxic metal ions are summarized in Table 1 . As it is seen from Table 1, excellent percent recoveries for both Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in the water samples displayed high capability of the pH-dependent ratiometric probe for determination of the mercury and copper ions in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the analytical merits of the ratiometric nanohybrid with previously reported fluorescent nanoprobres
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 summarizes the analytical merits (i.e., linear range, detection limit and selectivity) of the proposed dual-emissive NCs/CDs nanohybrid toward Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, compared with related fluorescent nanoprobes reported for simultaneous determination of these two ions in aqueous solution. From the table, selective determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by the reported probes is mostly relaied on masking of one of these ions with a masking agent for selective determination of the other one. In most cases, EDTA as an efficient Cu2+ chelating agent has been added to minimize the interference of the Cu2+ for selective detection of Hg2+ ions. However, this is in spite the fact that EDTA possesses high stability constants with both Hg2+ (log Kf = 21.5) and Cu2+ (log Kf = 18.8) and that the stability constant for the EDTA-Hg2+ is even much higher (i.e., by some three orders of magnitude) than that of the EDTA-Cu2+ complex. As, in Ref. [30], the percentage of fluorescence recovery after addition of EDTA to NCs quenched with Hg2+ ions was reported to be higher than that for NCs quenched with Cu2+ ions. Therefore, different stability constants of the EDTA complexes of these ions cannot satisfactorily explain the reported masking behavior of EDTA. According to our results, since Hg2+ ions interact with NCs, while Cu2+ ions interact with the surface Ins ligands, only Cu2+ ions can be removed from the NCs by EDTA, although its stability constant is lower.

Moreover, according to our results, although the addition of EDTA could not restore the fluorescence of NCs quenched by Hg2+, the presence of free EDTA in the NCs/CDs nanohybrid solution prevented the interaction of added Hg2+ ions with NCs. To solve this problem, we found that the NCs/CDs nanohybrid solution must be first incubated with the samples containing both Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions, and then EDTA be added to restore the quenched fluorescence arises from Cu2+ ions. It should be noted that, in this case, the amount of added EDTA is critical because its high excess amount can also restore the fluorescence quenched by Hg2+ ions, at least partly. Since the interfering concentration of Cu2+ in real samples is unknown, this can be an important source of error. In addition, eliminating the interfering effect of Hg2+ for selective determination of Cu2+ ions using this method is even more complicated.

On the contrary, in our proposed method, both Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions can be selectively determined just by controlling the solution pH, without using any additional chemical agent or any additional step. For example, the solubility product of Hg(OH)2 and, consequently, the free amount of Hg2+ ions at pH 10.0 is so small that cannot disturb the determination of Cu2+ ions.

In summary, bimetallic NCs using Ins as both capping and reducing agent were synthesized. These red emitting NCs along with blue emitting CDs were employed to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent nanohybrid probe for selective detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions, free from disturbance by other metal ions tested. The ratiometric probe showed a pH dependent dual-selectivity for Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions. This unique feature provided the possibility of switching the selectivity of the probe by simply adjusting the pH of the probe solution without using any additional agent.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of this work Razi University Research Council is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.12.104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
